output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.95, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.85}}

Critique of the introduction/related-work section for CalDML.

**1. The "first" / novelty claim is overreached and likely incorrect.**
The sentence "the first procedure that produces calibrated confidence intervals for CATE estimates obtained from arbitrary machine-learning nuisance learners" is a strong priority claim that the section does not defend. There is a substantial existing literature on conformal and split-conformal approaches to treatment-effect inference that is conspicuously absent — e.g., Lei & Candès (2021, "Conformal inference of counterfactuals and individual treatment effects"), Alaa & van der Schaar's conformal CATE work, Yin, Shi, Lei & Candès on conformal sensitivity analysis, Jin, Ren & Candès on sensitivity-robust conformal ITE intervals, and Chernozhukov, Wüthrich & Zhu on conformal prediction under covariate shift. Because CalDML is described as "split-conformal-style," the omission of this line of work is the single biggest weakness of the section. Either (a) soften the claim to "to our knowledge, the first DML-wrapper procedure that…," and explicitly contrast against Lei & Candès-style conformal CATE methods, or (b) drop the priority claim entirely and frame the contribution as a calibration wrapper specific to DML pipelines.

**2. The conformal validity argument as stated is too loose.**
The paragraph says coverage "holds whenever the calibration fold is exchangeable with the deployment distribution, irrespective of how fast the nuisance learners converge." This is the standard split-conformal guarantee for *predictive* intervals over an observed outcome. CATE is a function of two counterfactual outcomes, only one of which is ever observed per unit; you cannot conformalize it without an additional identification assumption (e.g., unconfoundedness, plus typically overlap, plus some surrogate score whose conditional distribution matches the CATE residual). Either the method actually provides intervals for some observable proxy (e.g., the doubly-robust pseudo-outcome) and then inherits CATE coverage only under additional assumptions — in which case say so — or the validity claim is overstated. As written, a careful reader will not believe the guarantee.

**3. Empirical claims are stated too cleanly to be credible without qualification.**
"Empirical coverage within 1.5 percentage points of nominal across all three benchmarks" while baseline DML "under-covers by 8 to 15 points" and width inflates by "less than 30%" — these numbers are presented as uniform across IHDP, ACIC-2018, and the Lalonde-style benchmark. In practice IHDP has very different DGP characteristics from ACIC-2018, and CATE coverage on Lalonde-derived data is notoriously fragile. Either report ranges per-benchmark, or note that these are aggregate/median figures. Also clarify whether coverage is marginal, conditional on X, or averaged over a held-out test grid — these are very different targets and the field has been burned by the ambiguity.

**4. Related-work coverage gaps beyond the conformal omission.**
- Semenova & Chernozhukov (2021) on debiased ML inference for best linear projections of CATE, and Foster & Syrgkanis (2023) on orthogonal statistical learning, are directly relevant rate-condition baselines.
- Nie & Wager (2021, R-learner) belongs alongside Künzel et al. and Kennedy.
- The Wager & Athey (2018) characterization is slightly off: their pointwise intervals require a particular subsample size schedule and honesty, not just "regularity conditions on the forest splits." Tighten the phrasing.
- Robins & Rotnitzky 1995 is the right ancestor for DR scores in missing-data contexts; for the CATE-relevant DR score, Robins, Rotnitzky & Zhao (1994) or van der Laan & Robins (2003) are more standard citations.

**5. Structure and framing.**
The piece reads as intro + related work fused. Two specific reorganizations would help: (a) move the "we frame this as a meaningful step beyond the existing toolbox" sentence — it's editorializing without content — and replace it with a one-sentence statement of *what* assumption CalDML trades away and *what* it adds. (b) Foreground the contribution before the related work block, then return to a contributions list at the end of the section that is explicit about: (i) the wrapper algorithm, (ii) the validity theorem and its assumptions, (iii) the empirical claim. Right now the contribution is implicit and the reader has to assemble it.

**6. Prose-level issues.**
- "slow-converging or biased on subpopulations" — these are different failure modes; separate them.
- "regardless of the convergence rate of the underlying nuisance models" appears twice in nearly identical form (paragraph 2 and paragraph 4); collapse.
- "leans on" is colloquial for a methods section; use "exploits" or "is based on."
- "We position CalDML as a practical default" — claim-by-assertion. Either remove or back with one sentence about computational cost / drop-in usage.
- "the Lalonde-style observational extension of Smith and Todd, 2005" — the standard citation for this construction is Smith & Todd (2005) in the *Journal of Econometrics*; verify the year and venue and whether you mean the LaLonde (1986) data as reanalyzed by Smith & Todd, which should be phrased accordingly.

**7. Reference accuracy to double-check.**
- Chernozhukov et al. (2018) — confirm whether you mean the *Econometrics Journal* DML paper; there are several closely related Chernozhukov 2017/2018 references.
- Kennedy (2023) — verify the year; the widely cited DR-learner preprint is 2020 with a 2023 journal version, and which you cite matters for reproducibility.
- Künzel et al. (2019) — PNAS, correct.

Recommended priority for revision: fix #1 and #2 first (they are correctness issues that a reviewer will flag immediately), then #3 (credibility of the empirical headline), then #4–#7.

Uncertainty disclosure: specific publication years and venues for Kennedy, Smith & Todd, and the Chernozhukov DML paper are stated from memory and should be verified against the actual bibliography. The list of missing conformal-CATE references in point #1 is representative, not exhaustive — a literature sweep may surface additional priority-relevant work.
